speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 2, interview with Selma Engel. You were telling us about the knapsacks that you had packed by the door. Did the directions that you got about them tell you what kinds of things to put in those knapsacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that we got it from the people was the Jewish rat. The Jewish rat is a-- 'rat' means advice-giving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told you exactly what to do. And I think they send us to Poland. They helped-- and they saved their own life. Because they know-- They didn't know, perhaps, what was going on in Poland, because we didn't have any radio, and perhaps they didn't know that was ga-- who knows that-- believed that you go to the gas chamber. But I think they know it was not so good to go to Poland. Because I remember that they talked about the concentration camps were very important in Germany. Now I remember that my parents talked about it, of course-- in concentration camps in Germany when they were there, and that was in Germany after the Jews. Yeah, now, the more I go into it, I remember that. And so they know, too. And I perhaps didn't read the newspaper. You know that there were concentration camps in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were forced to move out of your hotel and into this little house, were you were able-- did you-- did you still keep up friendships with non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. No. That was already in the time that we were not allowed-- that we are not allowed to go with them. And somehow a lot of my Jewish friends-- not-Jewish friends-- found out later, after the war, that they were all, um-- they call it in Holland [DUTCH]. They were all folk Germans. They went to the Germans! And I visit them after the war. And I didn't know even. Later on, I found out things like that. A lot of them!

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the beginning of the war, did they talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that we had any contact with not-Jewish people. Very little. I don't remember that-- the Jews really stuck together and visit each other. Although, they didn't tell them what they want to do-- hide in the war or not. They didn't tell each other that. That I don't remember. But, no, the Jews very much stick together-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1940-- beginning 1942, I think, perhaps 1941-- I don't know exactly the date-- all Jewish had to wear a star. And was 'Jew'-- 'Jude'-- written on. And when I was in that small house, I couldn't stand it. So I started at first helping in my hometown all the people what couldn't have help-- Jewish help-- anymore-- no, not Jewish help anymore. I started helping them. But that was not very satisfying. I hated it. Because they were all friends from my mother, and I had to clean the houses, and I didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to another town, which was not allowed, and I took my Jewish star off. What was dangerous, but I really think  I didn't see the danger. And I went by train to another town-- Apeldoorn. So, 50 kilometer from our-- my-- from Zwolle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was an older couple. And I helped this older couple, and I got some money for it, too. And they had a textile store. And then weekends I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find this older couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think perhaps somebody told my mother-- somebody from the family. We had a very large family. My mother had eight brothers and sisters, and my father had seven brothers and sisters. And my mother's family lived all in our area-- were not far, in north von Holland. So they talked to each other all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a Jewish couple that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was a Jewish couple-- an older couple. And I went there and helped there and went there. And was good. I went away. And had an aunt and uncle-- it was a doctor's family-- that lived also in Apeldoorn, and I could visit them. It was better for me, too, to get away from that small house, where my mother was sitting the whole day. My one brother had a girlfriend, and I think they were-- in between-- were married. My brother married. And so we were sitting-- and I thought it was very stuffy-- knitting the whole day-- and I just-- I was 19, and I went out and went to a family and helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, I was standing outside. And in the same street was a very large house, and a Catholic priest there. And that all that I really remember things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this Catholic priest came to me and said, how do you think that you can be safe-- that you won't be sent away, out of the country? And I-- I don't know how I came up with the idea say to him that the only way that a not-Jewish people take me in and, uh-- and that-- I am not there anymore-- I have nothing to do with the family. And then I'm out of there. So the same afternoon, he came-- or a day later-- I don't remember. I know he came to me, and he say, I have a family-- I know found somebody what will take you in. Did you know this priest before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I looked Jewish. I really do-- in Holland, especially-- I have a big nose, and I was a little bit darker and talking with my hands a little bit. I'm a real, typical Jewish person. Not seen, perhaps, in America, but in Holland I was. And I know that. Because that's the reason I didn't want to run away or didn't want to-- even when I have a chance to go away, I know I had to be more brought up different than-- In Holland, would've been-- I couldn't do anything outside the house. I looked typical Jewish. I know that. Perhaps not so here in America, but in Holland, for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he knows that-- and we lived also in a house that belongs to a Jew. And he lived next door to us, but had a nicer house, of course. So he rented sometimes out that where we lived now in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he noticed-- he saw me. I was young. And he came to me, and he say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came. He say, be ready tomorrow morning, very early. We had already no bicycles anymore. We're not allowed to-- the German took our bicycles. They always took everything what was a little bit valuable from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he say, I come with a bicycle. And-- what-- I think that I remember. And somebody will bring you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now, this was when you were in Apel--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Apeldoorn, there was only there-- I worked five days, and then I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dangerous what I did, because when the Germans, when they found out I took my star off, that will be the end. But I was a little bit-- I didn't see the danger. Perhaps I didn't want to see the danger, but I didn't see the danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came back, and he told me that he has a place for me. And so the next morning, or two days later, he came. And there came somebody. And it was coincident-- a man-- he was the teacher in the school where I went-- English teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought me to Utrecht. I took my backpack. And my mother was sitting there, and my brothers. And I say goodbye to them, and I never saw them again. That was the last time that I saw my family. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother approve of your going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear later that my mother fainted when I went away. She was very upset, because I was the only girl, and she know perhaps, know that I-- God knows she will never see me again. You didn't know what was going on. They had no idea about gas chambers. We know there was something very dismay-- very unusual-- something-- we didn't know. [DRINKS] In between-- I forgot to tell you-- my brothers had to go in a work camp. And they worked there the whole week, and on weekends, from the beginning, they came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the work camp they went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no name. It was just a work camp where the Dutch people went to introduce them to something will happen to them, you know? They came home weekends, so it sound not so bad. And so, also, what I later hear, after World One, I have one brother that was not so strong. Mauritz was his name. He had to go to that work camp. And Marthyn thought he could help him, perhaps. And he went, also-- free he went there. And so they went to the work camp. And I don't remember if they were home or not. I remember that my mother, of course, was home. And they went-- without bringing sometimes not let them not come home anymore-- after half year, after a month, of two months, they didn't let them come home anymore. So my mother was then, later, alone. But I was already gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I say-- then my mother realize, perhaps-- perhaps I never see her back again. And then she-- but somebody told me she fainted. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I went also away without a penny in my pocket. And I don't know how my mother could have done that. I went away without a penny in my pocket.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to that family, and I stayed with this family-- three, four months? I don't remember exactly a time. It was a horrible time, because I was the maid there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland, they have this stove that you have to heat with coal. And so I had to put the stoves on every day. I have to clean it out and had to clean the whole house and had to cook for them. And in Holland, in between was already-- the Germans took everything out of Holland. And see, I lived more in northern von Holland, over the middle von Holland, there was more food. But they lived more to the area von Amsterdam. And the Germans took, really-- already started taking everything out of Holland. And when I was hidden by these people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was 1942. There was not much food in Holland. [INAUDIBLE] these people were too cheap to buy food when there was not more food. But they were eating outside. They worked, all of them. It was a man and wife and a brother. And were very Christian. And every day they prayed, when they brought some food home. And I remember there was a bone. I never forget. It was a bone, and I had to cook this bone over and over again. And sometimes they have cats in the house, and the cats were playing with the bone. But I still had to cook that all over that. And then I had to make soup from that. And she'd say, see, there's still some fat on top from the bone. And also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a little bit bread in the house, sometime-- oh, yeah! There were apples, also, in the attic, and I slept in the attic. And I stole, always, an apple. I remember. And when I was very-- in the evening, when I went into bed, I eat an apple. And I was always very hungry. And there was bread-- sometimes I make a sandwich for me and put it under my pillow. But when I came in the bed, that I had something to eat, I felt terrible guilty to do that! But I didn't-- there was no food in the house, so I didn't get much to eat. Only the food that they brought every day home with them. So I was very, very hungry. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they treated me-- they treated me at all. I was-- it didn't come to anything. They had not much-- only when I was eating together with them, and then I went to bed. So I didn't see them the whole day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was very lonely. I couldn't talk with anybody. I could never come near to the window. I could never open the door. And I felt lower and lower.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what I did was, I was a cleaning maid there, and that all what I did. So, and was no radio. There was nothing. And it was-- You know, I don't remember how I felt. Perhaps I felt very sorry for myself. I don't remember. I remember that I was hungry. That, I remember. And that I stole an apple. And I put a bread under my pillow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there came-- the last week when I was there came another Jewish couple. He was a professor from a university with his wife. And they came there because [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know how they came there. And they had also a stove in their room, because they paid them a lot of money. I didn't pay anything, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I had to also clean that stove, and that was really-- the wife and a professor. She never did anything in her life before. All the Dutch people had a maid. My mother had a maid, for the hotel. But I never did anything. I never cooked. I never did anything. Because we had maids! In the hotel, for the hotel, but in between, I was a-- I never did anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, when I came there, I had to do-- cook-- also-- and I hated that, to do that for her. Why couldn't she do it herself? She was in the same situation as I was. So instantly I hate the whole people that they came there. And I had more work. So it was a very difficult time. How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months, four months. I don't really-- really, exactly the months. It was a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish couple friendly to you? Could you talk to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were above me. I was just a-- also, they felt that I was the maid. No, they didn't talk with me. Yeah, they talked a little bit with me, that they had a son and a daughter, and one son was married with a not-Jewish girl, or something like that. They were very assimilated now, in this time. They were Jewish, of course. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember much about that. I don't know-- I don't want to remember, all these years, what happened in that time, I think. And that's the reason-- eh, perhaps I felt too dumb, or-- I don't know. I just blocked it out perhaps. There are some parts that I remember. That the man told me, one day, that I can go and visit the neighbors. There was a Jewish family hidden. And I was so happy to go and talk with a Jewish family and go there. So these people upstairs were not long there. And somehow I had no connections with them-- somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to this Jewish family-- Next door.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next door. And I had a-- every Jew in Holland had a passport with J in that. And so I went next door. And I was happy to see some Jewish people, just ordinary people like I was. And the minute that I came in, the police came. The Dutch police. And what has now happened-- the people where I was hidden, they were traitors. But I didn't know. Because after me, 20-- I found out after the war, 28 Jews were caught there. And when Jews were hidden by non-Jews, the non-Jewish people were punished, too. They were also sent to concentration camp, mostly only the men. And none of this happened to these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came in there, into the house from this family-- that was the lawyer's family that took in this whole family-- they say-- and I say to them, oh, I'm a nurse, I come to help these people. But I didn't know what to do. So they say, do you have your passport? And I had a passport. The underground in Holland was pretty big. And I don't know who did it and what they did, but they took the J out. And he say, can I see your passport? And I said, oh, god. And he hold it to the light, so he could see the J was taken out. So I didn't have anything to say anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me where I was hidden. But I didn't want to tell him, because I know there was a Jewish family just came there. And when they had find out that I was caught, perhaps they will get out of it. I found after the war-- got a letter from these people, that they were alive, and they went away. But I found also they went over to another religion. They went to the Catholic religion over because-- after the war. So I didn't have anything. I was very against things like that, so I will have nothing-- no contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was the couple that you had to clean--

speaker: interviewee
text: To clean-- yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Absolute no names. I don't remember anything. I don't even remember-- and I was very friendly with these people. They came from Utrecht-- where I came, where I was caught. I don't remember even their name. I have to go under hypnosis to remember more things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyway, the police asked me where I was hidden, and I didn't want to tell. So a few days later, then we went to the police station. They took us all-- of that family. And they had a son that had very bad asthma. And also the man had also bad asthma. Very sick people [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Utrecht, and we were a week in the police station. And in the time in the week, we went to that home where I was hidden. And we went to the bed where I slept in, and he opened the bed, and I see a sandwich there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say to these people, see what kind of a girl she is? She stole your food. That, I never forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also was an apple in there too, under my pillow. I know there was laying some food. So it was very upsetting for me. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you in jail? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was later. I went to Utrecht, and we stayed in the police station--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in jail. That was not a jail. We stayed in a room together, all together. And the police-- the Dutch police tried to get me out. Of course, my brother was hidden in Zwolle, my oldest brother-- was hidden in Utrecht. Well, he was married, and he lived in Utrecht. And he tried to get me out. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know you were here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think through the underground he was hear from the police. He had, perhaps== he was in contact with the underground, because he was hidden there. I think. And then, through the police, and he tried to get me out. But I didn't want to go out. Because first, I visit my brother once, and he was living in a very small room with two little kids. They had, in between, two boys. They were hidden in the war. And my sister-in-law didn't know anything at all. I mean, she didn't know how to clean the boys or to do something for these boys or to do anything. And it was a mess. Oh, it was a mess.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, if I have to go to my brother, I'd rather be dead than to live with him in that little house. I didn't get along with my sister-in-law. I didn't know her that well. That was after the war. She was just-- didn't fit in with me at all. So, and I had no money. And so-- where will I go? I had no idea that the underground was then already strong. And I had already--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was hidden-- I forgot to tell you-- they caught my mother. My mother, on the way out, was preparing to hide somewhere. She was caught. And she was sent to Poland. She was sent away. And also my brothers and my sister-in-law, I know they were sent away. And I wanted to go to my mother. I thought, she's in a big camp-- I will find her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the only thing-- I didn't-- and I had no money, too. I thought, when they caught me in Holland, where will I go? I don't know where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to go to my mother. I wanted to go to my mother. And perhaps I thought I can help her. I can be together with her. And that's the only thing that I thought. And I could have got out. I could have got out, in the jail. Also, the head of the police, he want to always be very friendly with me and want to take me separate. He wasn't-- somebody for the Germans, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, who knows where I will come? You know, I was a 19-years-old girl. I was in between, I think, already 20-- almost 20. And the Germans were not so good. And many times, do you know, they tried to-- not the Germans, but the folks Germans. What did they try to do? Did they make advances to you? What are you saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had maybe times that the people made advances to me. You know? And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you said no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say no. I just made away that I shied way of that. You know? I was really, uh-- I was also raped in the hotel, once. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was very sick from it. And I couldn't tell anybody [INAUDIBLE]. I really don't know what really happened after that. But I know I was sick, and I was in the hospital. Not in the hospital-- the doctors and-- and then the whole family whispered what was going on. I didn't know. But I was, once, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: This man that we give so much money. I was 19, yeah. So, and I didn't know even what a man was. Nothing. So it was-- yeah. So I was very afraid, really, that something like that-- And, also, when they want to get me out of the police station in Utrecht, I want to go to my mother. I thought, where she is, I will be safer, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to, uh-- we went from there to Amsterdam. And we were in jail in Amsterdam. Now who's 'we'? The, uh, the fa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. Yeah, just people-- families, too. So I was in Amsterdam in jail. And we were together with a whole bunch of-- several-- eight women, in a very small area. And it wasn't-- You know, we were all Jews. And everybody told their story. I was the youngest. I was the only one that come from the country and didn't have any life experience whatsoever. And everybody told about themselves. When we came in the jail, there was a woman there also-- a Jewish woman. A very well-to-do woman. Her husband had a very big factory from hat factory. And she was beautiful. She had smuggled in all this to bleach her hair. And I came-- I was-- my mother knitted, always, everything for me. I had knitted knee socks and knitted overwear and knitted, you know, everything. My mother put me on that I should be warm. So I was really a country girl. And so everybody told all the stories about their life. And there was a doctor's wife, and there was a girl what was a beautician. And so there was a very interesting [INAUDIBLE] all these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get much to eat. But that woman what was already nine months in jail, she taught us how to take care of ourself. We had to do exercise and to eat very slow and things like that. You know? So we always talked and-- what I told you. We got very little food.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, one of the Germans brought us over the food-- say, you know, when you laugh, you sound like somebody that I met in a hotel-- what was working in a hotel as a waiter. And also your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she says, do you have a brother? I say, yeah, I had brother what was-- my brother had these children-- was in a hotel school and had to work in a hotel, from his-- before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she met him, my brother. So that was very good. And after that, we got double food, and she smuggled things in for us. So that was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was someone who really helped you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not really 'helped'-- she was a German, I think. But she gave-- because I was together my brother, she say she was not-- she saw it was not enough food what we got. And she gave us some double food for us in jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- we were in-- oh, yeah-- also, I smoked. Everybody smoked, in that time. And I remember I had cigarettes and no matches. And I was sitting, whole day, with the cigarette on the lamp, that it would start to burn, but it never burned. So I gave the cigarettes what I had also to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was really the only valuable things what I had, my few cigarettes. But I don't know how I got the cigarettes. But I didn't have anything valuable with me. I never had any valuable with me, had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in January. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was already Christmas time. We got some extra food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also an aunt from who lived in Amsterdam, and she heard that I was in jail. And she brought us-- she sent us packages with some, sent me some packages with some clothes. It was really not much special-- not with food or anything. That was, perhaps, not allowed. Also, that very rich woman with the blonde hair, she always smuggled papers and something like that out of the jail. When you have money, you could do a lot of things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've come to the end of this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm hungry. You're not?
